mappings.rationaleAI: Traces prenatal development, cell differentiation, growth, and ageing -- directly relevant to life cycle stages (A.2, A.4, A.6).
Directly covers immune response, defenses against disease, and inflammation (C.2.c, C.2.d).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers REM, NREM, sleep disorders, metabolism, and the relationship between sleep and health (D.3).
Directly covers dreaming, brain imaging during sleep, and psychophysiological variations in sleep (D.3.b).
Directly covers functions of food, classes of food, recommended nutrient intakes, obesity, and therapeutic diets (D.1).
Addresses diet, food safety, and obesity relevant to feeding behaviour and food functions (D.1.a, D.1.d).
Directly covers the aging stage of the life cycle, life expectancy, and health implications of aging (A.6).
Explores puberty and adolescent development as a key postnatal developmental stage in the life cycle (A.4).
Explains physiological mechanisms for maintaining health, homeostatic regulation of the internal environment, and bodily stress responses (C.1).
Details the alarm reaction hormones (adrenaline, cortisol) mediating fight-or-flight, and reproductive hormone cycles governing puberty and fertility (C.5, A.5).
Explains the role of blood in preventing hemorrhage through clotting cascades and haemoglobin's oxygen transport function (C.3).
Explores biological rhythms -- driven by the suprachiasmatic nucleus -- that are fundamental to sleep regulation, hormone cycles, and health maintenance (D.3).
Examines cellular adaptation to stress and stem cell biology relevant to tissue regeneration and repair (C.1, C.4).
Explains how pluripotent and adult stem cells drive tissue regeneration and wound healing processes (C.4).
Addresses exercise and physical conditioning, including health maintenance through physical activity (D.2).
The human microbiome plays roles in immune defense, nutrient absorption, and overall health maintenance (C.2, D.1).
Explores genetic factors relevant to congenital conditions and hereditary variation affecting the human life cycle from fertilization (A.1).
Establishes definitions of normality in health, identifies disease risk factors, and sets population health standards (B).
